Article.ArticleTitle: Making waves: Harnessing electroactive microorganisms for efficient and sustainable removal of emerging contaminants in wastewater treatment plants.

Abstract.AbstractText: Wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) face significant pressure in addressing the escalating load of emerging contaminants (ECs) in wastewater while simultaneously minimizing energy consumption to support the global goal of carbon neutrality. Building on established theories as well as our own findings and a number of relevant lab-scale studies, we propose a novel concept of electron trade, which harnesses electroactive microorganisms (EAMs) to recover energy in the form of electrons from organic matter, powering efficient EC removal in WWTPs. Specifically, EAMs oxidize the abundant organic matter in wastewater and deliver electrons to electrodes via extracellular respiration in the oxygen-free zone. These electrons are then directed to the oxic zones through an external circuit, reducing oxygen to synthesize hydrogen peroxide or even subsequent hydroxyl radicals that facilitate EC removal. Furthermore, a potential practical configuration is suggested to integrate electron harvesting and utilization into existing WWTP infrastructures. Although further evidence and systematic optimization are required to achieve large-scale implementation, this perspective highlights the feasibility and potential of the electron trade mechanism as a paradigm shift that prioritizes direct electron utilization to enhance EC removal, offering an eco-friendly and sustainable solution in WWTPs.
